params.text:
Keeping food out of the danger zone

Harmful bacteria multiply most quickly between 8C and 63C - this range is known as the danger zone. The aim of temperature control is to keep food either cold enough or hot enough to stay out of it.

  • Keep your fridge at 5C or below so chilled food stays safe.
  • Cook and reheat food to a core temperature of 75C to kill bacteria (reheat food only once).
  • Hold hot food being served at 63C or above.
  • Cool cooked food quickly - ideally within 90 minutes - then refrigerate it.

Use a clean, calibrated probe thermometer to check core temperatures rather than relying on guesswork.

params.taskDescription: Drag the correct figures into this temperature-control guidance.
params.textField: Harmful bacteria multiply fastest in the danger zone between 8 and *63* degrees Celsius. Keep your fridge at *5* degrees or below. Cook and reheat food to a core temperature of *75* degrees. Aim to cool cooked food within *90* minutes before refrigerating it.

params.question: It is safe to reheat the same portion of cooked food several times as long as it looks hot.

l10n.wrongAnswerMessage: Incorrect. Food should only be reheated once, and must reach a core temperature of 75C. Repeated cycles through the danger zone let bacteria multiply.
l10n.correctAnswerMessage: Correct. Food should only be reheated once, and must reach 75C right through. Repeated reheating and cooling increases the risk of food poisoning.
